text: The surgeon incises the posterior sheath of the rectus muscle lateral to the linea alba, identifying the rectus muscle below the incision. The retromuscular plane is developed to the lineus humulunaris, preserving the perforators to the rectus muscle. The posterior rectus sheath is incised medial to the perforators to expose the transversus abdominis muscle, allowing dissection off the perforators while maintaining their attachment to the rectus muscle.

The surgeon divides the transversus abdominis muscles entirely, dissecting the transversalis fascia from the posterior side of these muscles. The dissection extends from the arcuate line in the lower abdomen to the diaphragm and xiphoid in the upper abdomen. The defect measures eight by twelve centimeters, requiring complete dissection and release of the transversus abdominis muscles along the abdomen's length. The transversus abdominis is transected at its intersection with the diaphragm, and the transversalis fascia is released around port sites to preserve the posterior sheath's integrity. Blunt dissection continues laterally into the retroperitoneum, entering the preperitoneal space and extending to the diaphragm.

The surgeon incises the posterior rectus sheath on the opposite side, extending the dissection to the linea semilunaris. The medial aspect of the posterior rectus sheath is incised, and the transversus abdominis muscles are transected from the arcuate line to the diaphragm. The transversus abdominis is visibly transected at its intersection with the diaphragm. The dissection continues at the midline under the xiphoid, releasing subxiphoid fat to expose the central tendon of the diaphragm. Inferiorly, the dissection enters the space of Retzius to connect the two dissection planes.

The surgeon inspects the viscera and abdominal contents for injury before desufflating the abdomen slightly. The medialized posterior rectus sheaths are reconnected using two number two V-lock sutures, running superiorly to inferiorly and from the inferior aspect to the midline, and tied together to close the posterior rectus sheath and complete a visceral sac. Holes in the posterior sheath from previous port placements or dissection are closed using figure-of-eight sutures or clips. The space is irrigated, and the anterior defect in the anterior sheath is closed, re-approximating the linea alba with transfacial sutures in a figure-of-eight shoelace fashion.

The surgeon successfully approximates the linea alba without tension and places a 20x25 cm ventral light echo mesh into the retromuscular space, securing it with tacks. The balloon is removed through the port, followed by the removal of ports and closure of the skin. The patient is extubated in the operating room.
